coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of waterproofness and luminescence thermostability of K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+ by coating with GQDs and surface passivation
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Qinfang
Deng, Daishu
Cen, Xuelian
Li, Jinrong
Wu, Dongmei
Li, Yuelan
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
coredata.dc:description: Mn4+- doped fluoride red luminescence phosphor can effectively improve the white light quality of white light emitting devices (WLEDs), but its poor waterproofness and luminescence thermostability hinder its application. In this work, a strategy for synthesizing KSF-MLG-P red luminescence phosphor (KSF-MLG = K2SiF6:0.05 Mn4+, 0.04Li+@GQDs (6 mg/ mol), P = passivation, GQDs = graphene quantum dots.) is proposed with an incorporation of hydrogen peroxide solution-free hydrothermal synthesis, hydrothermal coating and surface passivation methods. The characterization results indicate that it has strong luminescence, luminescence thermostability and waterproofness. The enhancement mechanism of luminescence performances is also discussed. A prototype WLED was assembled by a mixture of phosphors of KSF-MLG-P and YAG:Ce3+ with an InGaN chip. It produces warm white light with favourable performances (CCT = 3828 K, Ra = 91.1 ± 2.0, luminescent efficiency = 101.8 ± 2.1 lm W−1). So, it could be a vital candidate for WLED uses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors
Water resistance
Luminescence thermal stability
Fluorescent thermal stability
Passivation strategy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region , China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022 , No. 2020GXNSFAA159036 ), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116 ), the i nnovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015 ), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. S202210593143 ).

This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022, No. 2020GXNSFAA159036), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. S202210593143).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of four samples ((i) KSF-M, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P: (a) Full patterns, (b) Local expanded patterns.
SEM and TEM images, EDS and XPS spectra of the samples, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P: (a–c) SEM images of (ii-iv), (d–f) EDS spectra, (g, h) XPS spectra, (i) TEM of (iv).
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of four samples, (ia) KSF:Mn4+ (x), (iia) KSF:0.05Mn4+, yLi+, (iia) KSF-ML@GQDs(z mg.mol−1), (iva) KSF-MLG-P(t).

Luminescent properties of four samples, (i) KSF-M, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P: (a), (b) (photoluminescence excitation) PLE and PL spectra, (c) CIE chromaticity diagram, (d) Lifetime curves of the samples (i-iv).

Water resistance test of the samples (ii-iv), (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P: (a) Relative emission intensity changing with different immersion times, (b)Photos before immersing under natural light, (c)Photos after immersing in deionized water under natural light.

Performances of prototype WLED using the mixing phosphors of KSF-MLG-P and YAG:Ce3+ with an InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current: (a) Electroluminescence spectrum, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram, (c) Photo of the WLED.
ICP element analysis results of samples (i-ii).

head.ce:title: Enhancement of waterproofness and luminescence thermostability of K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+ by coating with GQDs and surface passivation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources, Environment and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mn4+- doped fluoride red luminescence phosphor can effectively improve the white light quality of white light emitting devices (WLEDs), but its poor waterproofness and luminescence thermostability hinder its application. In this work, a strategy for synthesizing KSF-MLG-P red luminescence phosphor (KSF-MLG = K2SiF6:0.05 Mn4+, 0.04Li+@GQDs (6 mg/ mol), P = passivation, GQDs = graphene quantum dots.) is proposed with an incorporation of hydrogen peroxide solution-free hydrothermal synthesis, hydrothermal coating and surface passivation methods. The characterization results indicate that it has strong luminescence, luminescence thermostability and waterproofness. The enhancement mechanism of luminescence performances is also discussed. A prototype WLED was assembled by a mixture of phosphors of KSF-MLG-P and YAG:Ce3+ with an InGaN chip. It produces warm white light with favourable performances (CCT = 3828 K, Ra = 91.1 ± 2.0, luminescent efficiency = 101.8 ± 2.1 lm W−1). So, it could be a vital candidate for WLED uses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: WLEDs as a novel solid-state illuminant are extensively used in many fields, such as lighting, medicine, and traffic signals, and have far-reaching research value and great potential application prospects [1–4]. Warm white light WLEDs are characterized by low correlated color temperature (CCT<4000 K) and high color rendering index (CRI or Ra>80) [3–8]. The red luminescence phosphor can make WLEDs produce warm white light with the above-mentioned good properties [3–8]. Red luminescence phosphors of Mn4+-doped fluorides are ones of such important phosphors. Unluckily, some disadvantages of such phosphors have yet to be solved: (a) Their thermal stability of luminescence is poor. Affected by thermal quenching, the PL intensity of the phosphors will be seriously decreased at operating temperature of WLEDs (about 150 °C). (b) They have poor moisture resistance. The activator K2[MnF6] of the phosphor is easily hydrolyzed into hydroxides and oxides in a humid environment, and then strongly reduces the intensity of luminescence. (c) Their luminescence is weaker than that of yellow emission phosphor (YAG:Ce3+). The reason is that the luminescence of the former is a parity-forbidden d→d transition, while that the latter is a parity-allowed d→f transition [9]. Therefore, the research on the development of red emitting phosphors of Mn4+-doped fluorides with high luminescence, luminescence thermostability and waterproofness has both academic value and application value.

It is interesting that some of the phosphors have high fluorescence thermal stability at the operating temperature of WLEDs supported by the negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect [10–13]. They include CsMoO2F3: Mn4+ [10], K2SiF6:Mn4+@ Al2O3 [11], K2(Ge0.7Si0.3)F6: Mn4+ [12], Rb2TiF6: Mn4+ [13], and their luminescence intensities at 150 °C are stronger than those at room temperature. Furthermore, some of our previous reports revealed that NTQ effect could also be obtained after Mn4+-doped fluorides to be coated with GQDs, which resulted an improvement of the luminescence thermal stability of the samples [14–19].

In addition, through unremitting efforts, the researchers obtained the Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors with different syntheses methods [5–43]. For most of the synthesis methods, an activator source of K2MnF6 was prepared by using KMnO4 and H2O2 as starting materials. However, the reaction between potassium permanganate and hydrogen peroxide solution is too vigorous for industrial application. In order to facilitate industrial applications, synthesis method of the phosphors without using hydrogen peroxide solution as starting material has been developed [5–8]. Besides, some author reported that the co-doped with Li + can improve the emission of Mn4+ ions [20–22]. In our previous work [23–26], the enhancement of Mn4+ emission by co-doping of Li+ in Mn4+-doped fluorides (Na2TiF6:Mn4+, Li+ [23], Na2SiF6:Mn4+, Li+ [24,25], Na2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+-P (P: surface passivating) [26]) were also observed. Therefore, in this paper, co-doping of Li+ is continued to be used to enhance the luminescence of K2SiF6:Mn4+.

Generally, surface modification is an effective method to improve water resistance of the Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors. Methods of surface modification include:(a) surface coating of insoluble substances; (b) surface passivation. For example, K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 with high water resistance was obtained by method of surface coating [27]. In our previous report [18], K2TiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2TiF6 with double shell layers and excellent water resistance also prepared with the surface coating method. Wang group [28] reported that K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 was prepared with surface passivation method, which also had good water resistance. In our previous report [18], K2TiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2TiF6 with double shell layers and excellent water resistance also prepared by an integration of ion exchange and coating methods. But few studies have been reported for preparation of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphor with double layers of GQDs and matrix via an incorporation of the coating method and the passivation method.

As a part of systematic research, in this work, a strategy for passivating K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+@ GQDs is proposed. The details are as follows: First, the hydrogen peroxide solution free hydrothermal synthesis method was used to synthesize K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+. Then, K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+ was coated with GQDs by a hydrothermal coating method to obtain K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+@GQDs. Finally, K2SiF6:Mn4+,Li+@GQDs was passivated with H2O2 at room temperature to obtain passivated sample (KSF-MLG-P) with double layers of GQDs and matrix. The characterization results show that the luminescent intensity, luminescence thermostability and water resistance of KSF-MLG-P are improved by Li+ doping, use of GQDs, and surface passivation.

The content of the experiment and properties of GQDs (Here, Cl-contained graphene quantum dots are used) are described in Supplementary Materials (including: (a) experimental and methodology (Eqs. (S1–S6)), (b) equations of the NTQ (Table S1 and Eqs. (S7–S11)), (c) properties of Cl-contained Graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) (Table S2, and Figs. S1–S7).

Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP) element analysis results of three samples ((i) KSF-M, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG) are described in Table 1 . Table 1 illustrates that possible molecular formulae of the three samples are as follows: (i) K2SiF6:0.05 Mn4+(KSF-M), (ii) K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+,0.04Li+(KSF-ML), (iii) K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+,0.04Li+@GQDs 6 mg/mol (KSF-MLG). The results show that most of KMnO4 (83.33%) has been converted into K2MnF6, and the latter is further doped into K2SiF6 matrix by using hydrothermal method under 110 °C for reaction 10 h. The formation of KSF-M can be represented by Eqs. (S2, S3).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) test results of four samples ((i) KSF-M, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P) are shown in Fig. 1 . All the patterns in Fig. 1 are indexed and consistent with the standard data of PDF#75–0694 (cubic K2SiF6 phase with space group Fm
3 ‾
m (225)) (Table 2 ). The results show that the four samples have the same cubic K2SiF6 phase.

Fig. 1(b) shows local expanded XRD patterns of the four samples, and their cell volume size order are as follows: (iv)< (ii)< (iii)< PDF#75–0694 <(i). The results are consistent with those in Table 2. The diffraction peak position of the sample (i) is shifted to the left compared to PDF#75–0694, showing the expansion of the cell volume. Because the radius of the manganese (+4) ion (r = 0.53 Å, CN = 6) is larger than that of the silicon (+4) ion (r = 0.40 Å, CN = 6) [29], when part of the lattice sites of Si4+ ions is replaced by Mn4+ ions, the expansion of the cell volume of the sample (i) is induced. Similarly, the diffraction peak position of the sample (ii) is shifted to the right, indicating that the cell volume has shrunk. The reason is that the radius of lithium (+1) ion (r = 0.76 Å, CN = 6) [44] is smaller than that of potassium ion (r = 1.38 Å, CN = 6) [45], which results in cell volume reduction of the sample (ii). When coated with GQDs, the cell volume of the sample (iii) is slightly larger than that of the sample (ii), which may be attributed to interaction between GQDs and Mn4+. Because the passivation process removes part of Mn4+, the cell volume of the passivated sample ((iv)) decreases slightly.

Fig. 2 shows SEM, TEM images, EDS and XPS spectra (SEM, TEM, EDS and XPS are abbreviations of scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, respectively.) of (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG and (iv) KSF-MLG-P, which they are irregular particles with varying sizes. KSF-ML has an aggregated particle with a length of about 5–10 μm, and KSF-MLG is a spherical block with radius about 2–4 μm, indicating that KSF-MLG has smoother surface after the coating with GQDs. KSF-MLG-P is also a spherical block with radius about 2–6 μm, but its surface is a little rougher than that of KSF-MLG.

Fig. 2(d–f) are the EDS test results. Fig. 2(d–f) show that the three samples all contain K, Si, and F elements, while KSF-MLG and KSF-MLG-P also contain C element. Li is too light to be detected. There is signal of Mn for the KSF-ML, KSF-MLG. But, no signal of Mn was occurred for the KSF-MLG-P, indicating that Mn on surface of the KSF-MLG-P was removed by passivation process [31].

In order to confirm that Mn4+ on surface of KSF-MLG-P has been effectively removed, we used XPS to characterize KSF-MLG and KSF-MLG-P (Fig. 2(g and h)). Fig. 2(g) depicts that the two samples have elements of Li, K, Si, F, O and C. Fig. 2(h) is XPS high resolution test results of manganese for the two samples. In Fig. 2(h), the Mn 2p peak intensity of the KSF-MLG-P is significantly lower than that of the KSF-MLG, suggesting that surface of the KSF-MLG-P has no Mn4+ ion, which is mutually verified and supported by the results of EDS [17].

TEM of the passivated sample (KSF-MLG-P) is shown in Fig. 2(i). Fig. 2(i) indicates that KSF-MLG-P has a shell layer with thick of 20 nm on its surface, which is the matrix protection layer formed during the passivating process [31]. The formation of KSF-MLG-P can be illustrated by Eqs. (S4–S6).

In our previous work, we synthesized K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@ K2SiF6 [46] and K2SiF6:Mn4+, Na+@GQDs@K2SiF6 [47] by double-coating method. The result of Fig. 2(i) shows that GQDs and matrix double-coated sample can be synthesized by the combination of GQDs coating method and surface passivating method.

Fig. 2(i) also displays that the surface of KSF-MLG-P has elliptical plaques with a diameter of about 100 nm. The elliptical plaques may be formed by GQDs clusters, also indicating that GQDs does not completely cover the surface of the sample. FTIR spectra of Cl-GQDs, KSF-ML, KSF-MLG are shown in Fig. S1. The strong and sharp peak at about 1640 cm−1 for GQDs can be attributed to characteristic peak of CC stretching vibration [48]. Fig. S1 indicates that there are peaks at about 1640 cm−1 in spectra of KSF-ML and KSF-MLG, but the former peak is a weak one, and the latter peak is a strong one. The CO stretching vibration absorption peak of CO2 also presents at about 1640 cm−1 [48]. So, peak at about 1640 cm−1 for KSF-ML may belong to CO2 absorption from air, while the peak for KSF-MLG may be attributed to the peak overlap of CC stretching vibration of GQDs and CO stretching vibration of CO2. The results show that GQDs have been successfully coated on the surface of KSF-ML, which is sustained by our previous work [19].

The fluorescence spectra of four series of samples ((ia) KSF:Mn4+ (x), (iia) KSF:0.05Mn4+, yLi+, (iiia) KSF-ML@GQDs(z mg.mol−1), (iva) KSF-MLG-P(t)) are shown in Fig. 3 . The influences of four factors on the emission spectra are shown in Fig. 3(a–d). Inserts of Fig. 3(a–d) show that fluorescence intensity curves of four samples are all nonlinear curves with the highest points, which corresponding x, y, z ant t are 0.06, 0.04, 6 and 4. The optimal samples are (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+ (KSF-M), (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+,0.04Li+ (KSF-ML), (iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+, 0.04Li+ @GQDs (6 mg / mol) (KSF-MLG), (iv) KSF:0.05Mn4+,0.04Li+@GQDs (4 h) (KSF-MLG-P).
Luminous performances of the four samples ((i) KSF-M, (ii) KSF-ML, (iii) KSF-MLG, (iv) KSF-MLG-P) are depicted in Fig. 4 .

Fig. 4(a and b) describe that the four samples have the same spectrum patterns of PLE and PL (PLE and PL are abbreviations of photoluminescence excitation photoluminescence, respectively), which only their luminescent intensities are different, but their peak shapes and peak positions are basically unchanged. In Fig. 4(b), PL peaks centered at 598, 608, 613, 631, 635 and 648 nm can be attributed to the vibrational modes of [MnF6]2- [32,33]. According to Fig. 5(S), the wavelengths of emission spectra of GQDs are 60–70 nm longer than those of corresponding excitation spectra, respectively. But, Fig. 4(b) shows that there is no corresponding emission spectrum peak in the range of 527–537 nm, indicating that GQDs do not emit light under the excitation of 467 nm.

Fig. 4(a and b) reveal that ratios of luminous intensity for the four samples are (iv):(iii):(ii):(i) = 1.43:1.38:1.20:1.00, where (iv):(iii) = 1.04, (iii):(ii) = 1.15, (ii):(i) = 1.20. The results indicate that luminescence of the samples is improved by Li+ ions, GQDs and passivation, respectively. The enhancement effect on luminescence of Mn4+ indued by co-doping with Li+ has also been reported in some literatures [34,35]. For example, Mao et al. [34] reported that integrated PL intensity of Sr2YSb0.98O6:0.01 Mn4+,0.01Li+ was 2.55 times that of the control sample without co-doping with Li+. The enhancement mechanism of luminescence intensity of Mn4+ caused by co-doping of Li+ can be attributed to formation of Mn4+-Li+ pairs [34]. The reason is that Mn4+-Li+ pairs can prevent the harmful energy transfer between neighboring manganese (+4) ions, then results in improvement the luminescence and reduction of concentration quenching of Mn4+ ions [34]. It is interesting that not only co-doping of Li + can enhance the luminescence intensity of Mn4+, but also other monovalent cations (Na+, K+, NH4 +) [16,17,20–22,49,50] can enhance the luminescence intensity of Mn4+. The results show that co-doping of monovalent cations can effectively block the energy transfer through the non-radiative transition between Mn4+ ions [34,51].

The enhancement effect on luminescence intensity of Mn4+ initiated by coating with GQDs can belong to GQDs coating layer, which the layer increases the absorption of excitation light, and also enhances probability of the radiative transition from 2Eg to 4A2g, due to coordination between GQDs and Mn4+ that GQDs give electrons to Mn4+ ions [15].

The enhancement effect on luminescence of Mn4+ indued by co-doping with GQDs is supported by K2SiF6@Cl-GQDs (Cl-GQDs = Cl containing GQDs) [52] and K2SiF6@GE-GQDs (GE-GQDs = Green emission GQDs) [53] reported in our previous works. The luminescence intensity ratio of the coated sample to the uncoated sample are as follows: (a), K2SiF6@Cl-GQDs is 4.60; (b) K2SiF6@GE-GQDs is 2.55. The enhanced results of the two coated samples [52,53] are much better than that of KSF-MLG (1.15), indicating that different GQDs have different enhancement effects in different matrices. The results of the comparison show that the luminescence enhancing effect produced by GQDs coating on the matrix of K2SiF6 is much larger than that in the matrix of K2SiF6:Li+, and the reasons for the results need to be further investigated.

The improvement of emission intensity induced by surface passivation can be considered as the contribution of passivation layer. The reasons for the enhanced luminescence by the passivation layer are as follows [36,37]: (a) It eliminates surface defects of the sample and prevents energy transfer from the sample interior to the defects. (b) It reduces the probability of non-radiative transition on the surface of the sample, then increases the luminescence intensity.

The luminous intensity ratios of the four samples obtained from Fig. 4 (b) illustrate that the order of enhancement for luminescence intensity of Mn4+ is co-doping with Li+>coating with GQDs > surface passivation. Due to the superposition of the above three enhancement effects, the luminescence intensity of the sample (iv) is the strongest among the four samples.

Fig. 4 (c) is CIE color coordinates of the four samples. Fig. 4 (c) illustrates that the four points are already overlapping, showing the values of the color coordinates are not affected by co-doping with Li+, coating with GQDs and surface passivation.

Fig. 4 (d) shows luminescence lifetime curves of the four samples (Here, the nonlinear equation of lifetime (y
t = A1*exp(-x/τ) + y 0) has been transformed into linear equation (Ln(y
t -y0) = A1-x/τ)), which the results are: (i) 8.2 ± 0.16 ms, (ii) 8.4 ± 0.17 ms, (iii) 8.5 ± 0.17 ms and (iv) 8.6 ± 0.17 ms. The results of Fig. 4 (d) indicate that the fluorescence lifetime of the samples can be increased by Li+ ions, GQDs and passivation, respectively.

Internal quantum yields (IQY) of the four samples were tested and calculated by Eq. (S1) [54], which they were: (i) 91.5 ± 1.92%, (ii) 94.4 ± 1.98%, (iii) 97.5 ± 2.05%, (iv) 99.6 ± 2.09%, respectively. The test results show that IQY vaulues of the samples are improved by Li+ ions, GQDs and passivation, respectively.

In practice, operating temperature of high power WLEDs chips can be as high as 150 °C, so the luminescence thermostability of the red light phosphor is an important factor [10]. Luminescence thermal performances of the four samples are illustrated in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a–d) demonstrate that temperature significantly affects the luminescence intensity, but does not affect the location of emission peak.

Fig. 5(e) depicts integrated luminous intensity curves of the four samples, which they're all parabolas with downward openings. The results suggest that the luminescence of the four samples has NTQ effect [11,38]. Fig. 5(e) further shows that the corresponding temperatures for the highest points of the four parabolas are 180 °C. The differences of the NTQ effect between the four samples are obvious, which the intensities of the samples (i-iv) at 180 °C are 150, 193, 252 and 322% of those initial ones at 30 °C, respectively. Thus, luminescence thermostability order of the samples is: (i)<(ii)<(iii)<(iv). Great NTQ effect induced by coating of GQDs was also observed in K2SiF6@Cl-GQDs [52] and K2SiF6@GE-GQDs [53] reported in our previous works.

The NTQ effect is usually interpreted by the mechanism of phonon-induction, which with the increase of temperature, intensities of Stokes (Is(T))) and anti-Stokes (Ias(T)) radiation transition induced by phonon are expressed with Eqs. (S7) and (S8) [39,40]. It should be noted that the NTQ comes not only from the optical phonons, but also from the acoustic ones [55,56]. However, the acoustic phonon contributions become remarkable only at low temperatures and also only in the anti-Stokes process [55,56]. Because of this, such acoustic-phonon contributions have not been considered in the present calculation.

To study the mechanism, the curves of Is(T) and Ias(T) changed with temperature were plotted and described in Fig. 5(f). Fig. 5(f) indicates that the curves are agreement with the results of Eqs. (S7, S8) [39,40]. The results illustrate that mechanism of phonon-induction causes the NTQ effects for the four samples.

Chromaticity shift (ΔE) curves of the samples at different temperature can be determined by Eqs. (S9) [43], and depicted in Fig. 5(f). Fig. 5(f) shows that ΔE of the sample (iv) is lower than that of the sample (iii) at the same temperature. The results show that at about 150 °C (the operating temperature of WLEDs), the ΔE of the KSF-MLG-P is adequate for the practical application in WLEDs [41–43].

The activation energy (E
a ) denotes the thermal quenching energy barrier, which the bigger the E
a , the harder thermal quenching is. E
a values of the samples (ii, iv) are obtained with Eqs. (S10, S11) [43], and depicted in Fig. 5(g). Fig. 5(f) described that the E
a of the KSF-MLG-P is larger than that of the KSF-MLG, indicating that fluorescent thermal stability of the former is better than that of the latter.

TG test results of the sample (iv) is shown in Fig. 5(h). Fig. 5(h) illustrates that mass loss at 200 °C is 0.43%, which can be attributed to the removal of a small amount of adsorption water. The result shows that the KSF-MLG-P has good chemical thermostability.

Waterproofness of the samples (ii-iv) is shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) depicts curves of relative emission intensity changing with immersion times, which the order of waterproofness is: (iv)>(iii)>(ii). It is seen form Fig. 6(a) that when the soaking time reaches 360 min (6 h), the remaining luminous intensities are: (iv) 79%, (iii) 44%, (ii) 39%, respectively. The results of Fig. 6(a) show that: (a) Waterproofness of KSF-MLG-P is improved obviously after passivating, which can be attributed to protection of the passivation layer. (b) The water resistance of GQDs-coated samples is also slightly improved, which belongs to the formation of GQDs-coating layer (Fig. 2(i)).

Wang group [28] reported that K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 was prepared with surface passivating method, and H2O2 was also used as reducing agent. Experimental results showed that when soaking into water for 360 min (6 h), luminescence intensity of the K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 decreased from 100% to 76%, whereas that of the K2SiF6:Mn4+ reduced from 100% to 11%.

Obviously, the waterproofness of the KSF-MLG-P (79%) is better than that of K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 (76%) reported by Wang group [28]. The possible reasons are suggested as follows: (a) Difference in matrix. Matrix of the former is Li+ doped K2SiF6 (K2SiF6:0.04Li+), while that of the latter is pure K2SiF6. Fig. 6(a) shows that when the soaking time reaches 360 min (6 h), the remaining luminescence intensity of the KSF-ML (K2SiF6:0.04Li+,0.05Mn4+) is 39%, while that of the K2SiF6:Mn4+ reported by Wang et al. [28] is only 11%. (b) The difference in the shell. The former has double shell, which are GQDs coating layer and matrix protective layer formed after passivating, while the latter has only a single shell, which is matrix protective layer formed after passivating.

In our previous work, we synthesized K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2SiF6 [46] and K2SiF6:Mn4+, Na+@GQDs@K2SiF6 [47] by double-coating method. After soaking in water for 6 h, the residual luminescence intensities of the two samples(K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2SiF6 and K2SiF6:Mn4+, Na+@GQDs@K2SiF6) were 95% and 92%, respectively, indicating their water resistances were better than that of the KSF-MLG-P. The reason may be that the matrix protective layer obtained by double coating is much thicker than that obtained by surface passivation.

Fig. 6(b and c) are photos of the three samples before and after immersing in deionized water under natural light. Fig. 6(b) indicates that the three samples are all yellowish crystals, which from (ii) to (iv), the color of the samples gradually lightened. Fig. 6(c) show that after soaking into water for 480 min (8 h), the KSF-MLG-P is only slightly yellow in color, while the KSF-MLG and KSF-ML are brown, and color of the KSF-ML is darker than that of the KSF-MLG. This indicates that the results of Fig. 6(a) and (b) are mutually supported and confirmed.

Fig. 7 shows performances of prototype WLED under a drive current of 20 mA. Electroluminescence spectrum of the WLED is shown in Fig. 7(a), which the white light is a kind of trichromatic white light.

Fig. 7(b) is CIE color coordinates (0.3578, 0.3539) of the WLED, indicating that the point is in the white light zone. Fig. 7(c) is photo of the WLED, which emits warm white light. Other properties of the WLED obtained from the test are as follows: (a) the CCT (correlated color temperature) is 3828 K, (b) Ra (the color rendering index) is 91.1 ± 2.0, (c) Luminescent efficiency is 101.8 ± 2.1 lm W−1. The results illustrate that the KSF-MLG-P has good luminescence performances, which can improve the indoor lighting performance of WLEDs by reducing CCT and increasing Ra, and has good application prospects.

In summary, KSF-MLG-P has been synthesized by an incorporation of hydrogen peroxide solution free hydrothermal synthesis, hydrothermal coating and surface passivation methods. The results of the characterization tests indicate that it has strong luminescence intensity, luminescence thermostability and waterproofness. The details are as follows: (a) Its emission intensity is 1.43 times that of the KSF-M, (b) Its integrated emission intensity at 180 °C is 3.22 times of the initial one at 30 °C. (c) After immersing in water for 360 min (6 h), its emission intensity is 79% of its initial one, whereas that the KSF-ML is only 39% of its initial value. A prototype WLED was assembled by using KSF-MLG-P. It emits warm white light with favourable performances (CCT = 3828 K, Ra = 91.1 ± 2.0, luminescent efficiency = 101.8 ± 2.1 lm W−1). So, it can be an important candidate for WLEDs applications.

Qinfang Tan: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Daishu Deng: Investigation. Xuelian Cen: Investigation. Jinrong Li: Investigation. Dongmei Wu: Investigation. Yuelan Li: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
